doc/src/sgml/catalogs.sgml
1<!-- doc/src/sgml/catalogs.sgml -->
2<!--
3 Documentation of the system catalogs, directed toward PostgreSQL developers
4 -->
5
6<chapter id="catalogs">
7 <title>System Catalogs</title>
8
9  <para>
10   The system catalogs are the place where a relational database
11   management system stores schema metadata, such as information about
12   tables and columns, and internal bookkeeping information.
13   <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>'s system catalogs are regular
14   tables.  You can drop and recreate the tables, add columns, insert
15   and update values, and severely mess up your system that way.
16   Normally, one should not change the system catalogs by hand, there
17   are normally SQL commands to do that.  (For example, <command>CREATE
18   DATABASE</command> inserts a row into the
19   <structname>pg_database</structname> catalog &mdash; and actually
20   creates the database on disk.)  There are some exceptions for
21   particularly esoteric operations, but many of those have been made
22   available as SQL commands over time, and so the need for direct manipulation
23   of the system catalogs is ever decreasing.
24  </para>
25
26 <sect1 id="catalogs-overview">
27  <title>Overview</title>
28
29  <para>
30   <xref linkend="catalog-table"/> lists the system catalogs.
31   More detailed documentation of each catalog follows below.
32  </para>
33
34  <para>
35   Most system catalogs are copied from the template database during
36   database creation and are thereafter database-specific. A few
37   catalogs are physically shared across all databases in a cluster;
38   these are noted in the descriptions of the individual catalogs.
39  </para>
40
41  <table id="catalog-table">
42   <title>System Catalogs</title>
43
44   <tgroup cols="2">
45    <thead>
46     <row>
47      <entry>Catalog Name</entry>
48      <entry>Purpose</entry>
49     </row>
50    </thead>
51
52    <tbody>
53     <row>
54      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-aggregate"><structname>pg_aggregate</structname></link></entry>
55      <entry>aggregate functions</entry>
56     </row>
57
58     <row>
59      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-am"><structname>pg_am</structname></link></entry>
60      <entry>relation access methods</entry>
61     </row>
62
63     <row>
64      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-amop"><structname>pg_amop</structname></link></entry>
65      <entry>access method operators</entry>
66     </row>
67
68     <row>
69      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-amproc"><structname>pg_amproc</structname></link></entry>
70      <entry>access method support functions</entry>
71     </row>
72
73     <row>
74      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-attrdef"><structname>pg_attrdef</structname></link></entry>
75      <entry>column default values</entry>
76     </row>
77
78     <row>
79      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-attribute"><structname>pg_attribute</structname></link></entry>
80      <entry>table columns (<quote>attributes</quote>)</entry>
81     </row>
82
83     <row>
84      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-authid"><structname>pg_authid</structname></link></entry>
85      <entry>authorization identifiers (roles)</entry>
86     </row>
87
88     <row>
89      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-auth-members"><structname>pg_auth_members</structname></link></entry>
90      <entry>authorization identifier membership relationships</entry>
91     </row>
92
93     <row>
94      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-cast"><structname>pg_cast</structname></link></entry>
95      <entry>casts (data type conversions)</entry>
96     </row>
97
98     <row>
99      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link></entry>
100      <entry>tables, indexes, sequences, views (<quote>relations</quote>)</entry>
101     </row>
102
103     <row>
104      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-collation"><structname>pg_collation</structname></link></entry>
105      <entry>collations (locale information)</entry>
106     </row>
107
108     <row>
109      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-constraint"><structname>pg_constraint</structname></link></entry>
110      <entry>check constraints, unique constraints, primary key constraints, foreign key constraints</entry>
111     </row>
112
113     <row>
114      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-conversion"><structname>pg_conversion</structname></link></entry>

116     </row>
117
118     <row>
119      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-database"><structname>pg_database</structname></link></entry>
120      <entry>databases within this database cluster</entry>
121     </row>

123     <row>
124      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-db-role-setting"><structname>pg_db_role_setting</structname></link></entry>

348     <row>
349      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-ts-dict"><structname>pg_ts_dict</structname></link></entry>
350      <entry>text search dictionaries</entry>
351     </row>

353     <row>
354      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-ts-parser"><structname>pg_ts_parser</structname></link></entry>
355      <entry>text search parsers</entry>

358     <row>
359      <entry><link linkend="catalog-pg-ts-template"><structname>pg_ts_template</structname></link></entry>

373   </tgroup>
374  </table>

376
377
378 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-aggregate">
379  <title><structname>pg_aggregate</structname></title>
380
381  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-aggregate">
382   <primary>pg_aggregate</primary>
383  </indexterm>
384
385  <para>
386   The catalog <structname>pg_aggregate</structname> stores information about
387   aggregate functions.  An aggregate function is a function that
388   operates on a set of values (typically one column from each row
389   that matches a query condition) and returns a single value computed
390   from all these values.  Typical aggregate functions are
391   <function>sum</function>, <function>count</function>, and
392   <function>max</function>.  Each entry in
393   <structname>pg_aggregate</structname> is an extension of an entry
394   in <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.
395   The <structname>pg_proc</structname> entry carries the aggregate's name,
396   input and output data types, and other information that is similar to
397   ordinary functions.
398  </para>
399
400  <table>
401   <title><structname>pg_aggregate</structname> Columns</title>
402   <tgroup cols="1">
403    <thead>
404     <row>
405      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
406       Column Type
407      </para>
408      <para>
409       Description
410      </para></entry>
411     </row>
412    </thead>
413
414    <tbody>
415     <row>
416      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
417       <structfield>aggfnoid</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
418       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
419      </para>
420      <para>
421       <structname>pg_proc</structname> OID of the aggregate function
422      </para></entry>
423     </row>
424
425     <row>
426      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
427       <structfield>aggkind</structfield> <type>char</type>
428      </para>
429      <para>
430       Aggregate kind:
431       <literal>n</literal> for <quote>normal</quote> aggregates,
432       <literal>o</literal> for <quote>ordered-set</quote> aggregates, or
433       <literal>h</literal> for <quote>hypothetical-set</quote> aggregates
434      </para></entry>
435     </row>
436
437     <row>
438      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
439       <structfield>aggnumdirectargs</structfield> <type>int2</type>
440      </para>
441      <para>
442       Number of direct (non-aggregated) arguments of an ordered-set or
443       hypothetical-set aggregate, counting a variadic array as one argument.
444       If equal to <structfield>pronargs</structfield>, the aggregate must be variadic
445       and the variadic array describes the aggregated arguments as well as
446       the final direct arguments.
447       Always zero for normal aggregates.
448      </para></entry>
449     </row>
450
451     <row>
452      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
453       <structfield>aggtransfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
454       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
455      </para>
456      <para>
457       Transition function
458      </para></entry>
459     </row>
460
461     <row>
462      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
463       <structfield>aggfinalfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
464       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
465      </para>
466      <para>
467       Final function (zero if none)
468      </para></entry>
469     </row>
470
471     <row>
472      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
473       <structfield>aggcombinefn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
474       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
475      </para>
476      <para>
477       Combine function (zero if none)
478      </para></entry>
479     </row>
480
481     <row>
482      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
483       <structfield>aggserialfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
484       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
485      </para>
486      <para>
487       Serialization function (zero if none)
488      </para></entry>
489     </row>
490
491     <row>
492      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
493       <structfield>aggdeserialfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
494       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
495      </para>
496      <para>
497       Deserialization function (zero if none)
498      </para></entry>
499     </row>
500
501     <row>
502      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
503       <structfield>aggmtransfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
504       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
505      </para>
506      <para>
507       Forward transition function for moving-aggregate mode (zero if none)
508      </para></entry>
509     </row>
510
511     <row>
512      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
513       <structfield>aggminvtransfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
514       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
515      </para>
516      <para>
517       Inverse transition function for moving-aggregate mode (zero if none)
518      </para></entry>
519     </row>
520
521     <row>
522      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
523       <structfield>aggmfinalfn</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
524       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
525      </para>
526      <para>
527       Final function for moving-aggregate mode (zero if none)
528      </para></entry>
529     </row>
530
531     <row>
532      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
533       <structfield>aggfinalextra</structfield> <type>bool</type>
534      </para>
535      <para>
536       True to pass extra dummy arguments to <structfield>aggfinalfn</structfield>
537      </para></entry>
538     </row>
539
540     <row>
541      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
542       <structfield>aggmfinalextra</structfield> <type>bool</type>
543      </para>
544      <para>
545       True to pass extra dummy arguments to <structfield>aggmfinalfn</structfield>
546      </para></entry>
547     </row>
548
549     <row>
550      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
551       <structfield>aggfinalmodify</structfield> <type>char</type>
552      </para>
553      <para>
554       Whether <structfield>aggfinalfn</structfield> modifies the
555       transition state value:
556       <literal>r</literal> if it is read-only,
557       <literal>s</literal> if the <structfield>aggtransfn</structfield>
558       cannot be applied after the <structfield>aggfinalfn</structfield>, or
559       <literal>w</literal> if it writes on the value
560      </para></entry>
561     </row>
562
563     <row>
564      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
565       <structfield>aggmfinalmodify</structfield> <type>char</type>
566      </para>
567      <para>
568       Like <structfield>aggfinalmodify</structfield>, but for
569       the <structfield>aggmfinalfn</structfield>
570      </para></entry>
571     </row>
572
573     <row>
574      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
575       <structfield>aggsortop</structfield> <type>oid</type>
576       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-operator"><structname>pg_operator</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
577      </para>
578      <para>
579       Associated sort operator (zero if none)
580      </para></entry>
581     </row>
582
583     <row>
584      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
585       <structfield>aggtranstype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
586       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
587      </para>
588      <para>
589       Data type of the aggregate function's internal transition (state) data
590      </para></entry>
591     </row>
592
593     <row>
594      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
595       <structfield>aggtransspace</structfield> <type>int4</type>
596      </para>
597      <para>
598       Approximate average size (in bytes) of the transition state
599       data, or zero to use a default estimate
600      </para></entry>
601     </row>
602
603     <row>
604      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
605       <structfield>aggmtranstype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
606       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
607      </para>
608      <para>
609       Data type of the aggregate function's internal transition (state)
610       data for moving-aggregate mode (zero if none)
611      </para></entry>
612     </row>
613
614     <row>
615      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
616       <structfield>aggmtransspace</structfield> <type>int4</type>
617      </para>
618      <para>
619       Approximate average size (in bytes) of the transition state data
620       for moving-aggregate mode, or zero to use a default estimate
621      </para></entry>
622     </row>
623
624     <row>
625      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
626       <structfield>agginitval</structfield> <type>text</type>
627      </para>
628      <para>
629       The initial value of the transition state.  This is a text
630       field containing the initial value in its external string
631       representation.  If this field is null, the transition state
632       value starts out null.
633      </para></entry>
634     </row>
635
636     <row>
637      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
638       <structfield>aggminitval</structfield> <type>text</type>
639      </para>
640      <para>
641       The initial value of the transition state for moving-aggregate mode.
642       This is a text field containing the initial value in its external
643       string representation.  If this field is null, the transition state
644       value starts out null.
645      </para></entry>
646     </row>
647    </tbody>
648   </tgroup>
649  </table>
650
651  <para>
652   New aggregate functions are registered with the <link
653   linkend="sql-createaggregate"><command>CREATE AGGREGATE</command></link>
654   command.  See <xref linkend="xaggr"/> for more information about
655   writing aggregate functions and the meaning of the transition
656   functions, etc.
657  </para>
658
659 </sect1>
660
661
662 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-am">
663  <title><structname>pg_am</structname></title>
664
665  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-am">
666   <primary>pg_am</primary>
667  </indexterm>
668
669  <para>
670   The catalog <structname>pg_am</structname> stores information about
671   relation access methods.  There is one row for each access method supported
672   by the system.
673   Currently, only tables and indexes have access methods. The requirements for table
674   and index access methods are discussed in detail in <xref linkend="tableam"/> and
675   <xref linkend="indexam"/> respectively.
676  </para>
677
678  <table>
679   <title><structname>pg_am</structname> Columns</title>
680   <tgroup cols="1">
681    <thead>
682     <row>
683      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
684       Column Type
685      </para>
686      <para>
687       Description
688      </para></entry>
689     </row>
690    </thead>
691
692    <tbody>
693     <row>
694      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
695       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
696      </para>
697      <para>
698       Row identifier
699      </para></entry>
700     </row>
701
702     <row>
703      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
704       <structfield>amname</structfield> <type>name</type>
705      </para>
706      <para>
707       Name of the access method
708      </para></entry>
709     </row>
710
711     <row>
712      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
713       <structfield>amhandler</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
714       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
715      </para>
716      <para>
717       OID of a handler function that is responsible for supplying information
718       about the access method
719      </para></entry>
720     </row>
721
722     <row>
723      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
724       <structfield>amtype</structfield> <type>char</type>
725      </para>
726      <para>
727       <literal>t</literal> = table (including materialized views),
728       <literal>i</literal> = index.
729      </para></entry>
730     </row>
731    </tbody>
732   </tgroup>
733  </table>
734
735  <note>
736   <para>
737    Before <productname>PostgreSQL</productname> 9.6, <structname>pg_am</structname>
738    contained many additional columns representing properties of index access
739    methods.  That data is now only directly visible at the C code level.
740    However, <function>pg_index_column_has_property()</function> and related
741    functions have been added to allow SQL queries to inspect index access
742    method properties; see <xref linkend="functions-info-catalog-table"/>.
743   </para>
744  </note>
745
746 </sect1>
747
748
749 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-amop">
750  <title><structname>pg_amop</structname></title>
751
752  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-amop">
753   <primary>pg_amop</primary>
754  </indexterm>
755
756  <para>
757   The catalog <structname>pg_amop</structname> stores information about
758   operators associated with access method operator families.  There is one
759   row for each operator that is a member of an operator family.  A family
760   member can be either a <firstterm>search</firstterm> operator or an
761   <firstterm>ordering</firstterm> operator.  An operator
762   can appear in more than one family, but cannot appear in more than one
763   search position nor more than one ordering position within a family.
764   (It is allowed, though unlikely, for an operator to be used for both
765   search and ordering purposes.)
766  </para>
767
768  <table>
769   <title><structname>pg_amop</structname> Columns</title>
770   <tgroup cols="1">
771    <thead>
772     <row>
773      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
774       Column Type
775      </para>
776      <para>
777       Description
778      </para></entry>
779     </row>
780    </thead>
781
782    <tbody>
783     <row>
784      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
785       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
786      </para>
787      <para>
788       Row identifier
789      </para></entry>
790     </row>
791
792     <row>
793      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
794       <structfield>amopfamily</structfield> <type>oid</type>
795       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-opfamily"><structname>pg_opfamily</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
796      </para>
797      <para>
798       The operator family this entry is for
799      </para></entry>
800     </row>
801
802     <row>
803      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
804       <structfield>amoplefttype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
805       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
806      </para>
807      <para>
808       Left-hand input data type of operator
809      </para></entry>
810     </row>
811
812     <row>
813      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
814       <structfield>amoprighttype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
815       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
816      </para>
817      <para>
818       Right-hand input data type of operator
819      </para></entry>
820     </row>
821
822     <row>
823      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
824       <structfield>amopstrategy</structfield> <type>int2</type>
825      </para>
826      <para>
827       Operator strategy number
828      </para></entry>
829     </row>
830
831     <row>
832      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
833       <structfield>amoppurpose</structfield> <type>char</type>
834      </para>
835      <para>
836       Operator purpose, either <literal>s</literal> for search or
837       <literal>o</literal> for ordering
838      </para></entry>
839     </row>
840
841     <row>
842      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
843       <structfield>amopopr</structfield> <type>oid</type>
844       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-operator"><structname>pg_operator</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
845      </para>
846      <para>
847       OID of the operator
848      </para></entry>
849     </row>
850
851     <row>
852      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
853       <structfield>amopmethod</structfield> <type>oid</type>
854       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-am"><structname>pg_am</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
855      </para>
856      <para>
857       Index access method operator family is for
858      </para></entry>
859     </row>
860
861     <row>
862      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
863       <structfield>amopsortfamily</structfield> <type>oid</type>
864       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-opfamily"><structname>pg_opfamily</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
865      </para>
866      <para>
867       The B-tree operator family this entry sorts according to, if an
868       ordering operator; zero if a search operator
869      </para></entry>
870     </row>
871    </tbody>
872   </tgroup>
873  </table>
874
875  <para>
876   A <quote>search</quote> operator entry indicates that an index of this operator
877   family can be searched to find all rows satisfying
878   <literal>WHERE</literal>
879   <replaceable>indexed_column</replaceable>
880   <replaceable>operator</replaceable>
881   <replaceable>constant</replaceable>.
882   Obviously, such an operator must return <type>boolean</type>, and its left-hand input
883   type must match the index's column data type.
884  </para>
885
886  <para>
887   An <quote>ordering</quote> operator entry indicates that an index of this
888   operator family can be scanned to return rows in the order represented by
889   <literal>ORDER BY</literal>
890   <replaceable>indexed_column</replaceable>
891   <replaceable>operator</replaceable>
892   <replaceable>constant</replaceable>.
893   Such an operator could return any sortable data type, though again
894   its left-hand input type must match the index's column data type.
895   The exact semantics of the <literal>ORDER BY</literal> are specified by the
896   <structfield>amopsortfamily</structfield> column, which must reference
897   a B-tree operator family for the operator's result type.
898  </para>
899
900  <note>
901   <para>
902    At present, it's assumed that the sort order for an ordering operator
903    is the default for the referenced operator family, i.e., <literal>ASC NULLS
904    LAST</literal>.  This might someday be relaxed by adding additional columns
905    to specify sort options explicitly.
906   </para>
907  </note>
908
909  <para>
910   An entry's <structfield>amopmethod</structfield> must match the
911   <structfield>opfmethod</structfield> of its containing operator family (including
912   <structfield>amopmethod</structfield> here is an intentional denormalization of the
913   catalog structure for performance reasons).  Also,
914   <structfield>amoplefttype</structfield> and <structfield>amoprighttype</structfield> must match
915   the <structfield>oprleft</structfield> and <structfield>oprright</structfield> fields of the
916   referenced <link linkend="catalog-pg-operator"><structname>pg_operator</structname></link> entry.
917  </para>
918
919 </sect1>
920
921
922 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-amproc">
923  <title><structname>pg_amproc</structname></title>
924
925  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-amproc">
926   <primary>pg_amproc</primary>
927  </indexterm>
928
929  <para>
930   The catalog <structname>pg_amproc</structname> stores information about
931   support functions associated with access method operator families.  There
932   is one row for each support function belonging to an operator family.
933  </para>
934
935  <table>
936   <title><structname>pg_amproc</structname> Columns</title>
937   <tgroup cols="1">
938    <thead>
939     <row>
940      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
941       Column Type
942      </para>
943      <para>
944       Description
945      </para></entry>
946     </row>
947    </thead>
948
949    <tbody>
950     <row>
951      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
952       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
953      </para>
954      <para>
955       Row identifier
956      </para></entry>
957     </row>
958
959     <row>
960      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
961       <structfield>amprocfamily</structfield> <type>oid</type>
962       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-opfamily"><structname>pg_opfamily</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
963      </para>
964      <para>
965       The operator family this entry is for
966      </para></entry>
967     </row>
968
969     <row>
970      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
971       <structfield>amproclefttype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
972       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
973      </para>
974      <para>
975       Left-hand input data type of associated operator
976      </para></entry>
977     </row>
978
979     <row>
980      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
981       <structfield>amprocrighttype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
982       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
983      </para>
984      <para>
985       Right-hand input data type of associated operator
986      </para></entry>
987     </row>
988
989     <row>
990      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
991       <structfield>amprocnum</structfield> <type>int2</type>
992      </para>
993      <para>
994       Support function number
995      </para></entry>
996     </row>
997
998     <row>
999      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1000       <structfield>amproc</structfield> <type>regproc</type>
1001       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1002      </para>
1003      <para>
1004       OID of the function
1005      </para></entry>
1006     </row>
1007    </tbody>
1008   </tgroup>
1009  </table>
1010
1011  <para>
1012   The usual interpretation of the
1013   <structfield>amproclefttype</structfield> and <structfield>amprocrighttype</structfield> fields
1014   is that they identify the left and right input types of the operator(s)
1015   that a particular support function supports.  For some access methods
1016   these match the input data type(s) of the support function itself, for
1017   others not.  There is a notion of <quote>default</quote> support functions for
1018   an index, which are those with <structfield>amproclefttype</structfield> and
1019   <structfield>amprocrighttype</structfield> both equal to the index operator class's
1020   <structfield>opcintype</structfield>.
1021  </para>
1022
1023 </sect1>
1024
1025
1026 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-attrdef">
1027  <title><structname>pg_attrdef</structname></title>
1028
1029  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-attrdef">
1030   <primary>pg_attrdef</primary>
1031  </indexterm>
1032
1033  <para>
1034   The catalog <structname>pg_attrdef</structname> stores column default
1035   expressions and generation expressions.  The main information about columns
1036   is stored in <link
1037   linkend="catalog-pg-attribute"><structname>pg_attribute</structname></link>.
1038   Only columns for which a default expression or generation expression has
1039   been explicitly set will have an entry here.
1040  </para>
1041
1042  <table>
1043   <title><structname>pg_attrdef</structname> Columns</title>
1044   <tgroup cols="1">
1045    <thead>
1046     <row>
1047      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1048       Column Type
1049      </para>
1050      <para>
1051       Description
1052      </para></entry>
1053     </row>
1054    </thead>
1055
1056    <tbody>
1057     <row>
1058      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1059       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1060      </para>
1061      <para>
1062       Row identifier
1063      </para></entry>
1064     </row>
1065
1066     <row>
1067      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1068       <structfield>adrelid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1069       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1070      </para>
1071      <para>
1072       The table this column belongs to
1073      </para></entry>
1074     </row>
1075
1076     <row>
1077      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1078       <structfield>adnum</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1079       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-attribute"><structname>pg_attribute</structname></link>.<structfield>attnum</structfield>)
1080      </para>
1081      <para>
1082       The number of the column
1083      </para></entry>
1084     </row>
1085
1086     <row>
1087      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1088       <structfield>adbin</structfield> <type>pg_node_tree</type>
1089      </para>
1090      <para>
1091       The column default or generation expression, in <function>nodeToString()</function>
1092       representation.  Use <literal>pg_get_expr(adbin, adrelid)</literal> to
1093       convert it to an SQL expression.
1094      </para></entry>
1095     </row>
1096    </tbody>
1097   </tgroup>
1098  </table>
1099 </sect1>
1100
1101
1102 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-attribute">
1103  <title><structname>pg_attribute</structname></title>
1104
1105  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-attribute">
1106   <primary>pg_attribute</primary>
1107  </indexterm>
1108
1109  <para>
1110   The catalog <structname>pg_attribute</structname> stores information about
1111   table columns.  There will be exactly one
1112   <structname>pg_attribute</structname> row for every column in every
1113   table in the database.  (There will also be attribute entries for
1114   indexes, and indeed all objects that have
1115   <link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link>
1116   entries.)
1117  </para>
1118
1119  <para>
1120   The term attribute is equivalent to column and is used for
1121   historical reasons.
1122  </para>
1123
1124  <table>
1125   <title><structname>pg_attribute</structname> Columns</title>
1126   <tgroup cols="1">
1127    <thead>
1128     <row>
1129      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1130       Column Type
1131      </para>
1132      <para>
1133       Description
1134      </para></entry>
1135     </row>
1136    </thead>
1137
1138    <tbody>
1139     <row>
1140      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1141       <structfield>attrelid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1142       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1143      </para>
1144      <para>
1145       The table this column belongs to
1146      </para></entry>
1147     </row>
1148
1149     <row>
1150      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1151       <structfield>attname</structfield> <type>name</type>
1152      </para>
1153      <para>
1154       The column name
1155      </para></entry>
1156     </row>
1157
1158     <row>
1159      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1160       <structfield>atttypid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1161       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1162      </para>
1163      <para>
1164       The data type of this column (zero for a dropped column)
1165      </para></entry>
1166     </row>
1167
1168     <row>
1169      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1170       <structfield>attlen</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1171      </para>
1172      <para>
1173       A copy of <literal>pg_type.typlen</literal> of this column's
1174       type
1175      </para></entry>
1176     </row>
1177
1178     <row>
1179      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1180       <structfield>attnum</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1181      </para>
1182      <para>
1183       The number of the column.  Ordinary columns are numbered from 1
1184       up.  System columns, such as <structfield>ctid</structfield>,
1185       have (arbitrary) negative numbers.
1186      </para></entry>
1187     </row>
1188
1189     <row>
1190      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1191       <structfield>atttypmod</structfield> <type>int4</type>
1192      </para>
1193      <para>
1194       <structfield>atttypmod</structfield> records type-specific data
1195       supplied at table creation time (for example, the maximum
1196       length of a <type>varchar</type> column).  It is passed to
1197       type-specific input functions and length coercion functions.
1198       The value will generally be -1 for types that do not need <structfield>atttypmod</structfield>.
1199      </para></entry>
1200     </row>
1201
1202     <row>
1203      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1204       <structfield>attndims</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1205      </para>
1206      <para>
1207       Number of dimensions, if the column is an array type; otherwise 0.
1208       (Presently, the number of dimensions of an array is not enforced,
1209       so any nonzero value effectively means <quote>it's an array</quote>.)
1210      </para></entry>
1211     </row>
1212
1213     <row>
1214      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1215       <structfield>attbyval</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1216      </para>
1217      <para>
1218       A copy of <literal>pg_type.typbyval</literal> of this column's type
1219      </para></entry>
1220     </row>
1221
1222     <row>
1223      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1224       <structfield>attalign</structfield> <type>char</type>
1225      </para>
1226      <para>
1227       A copy of <literal>pg_type.typalign</literal> of this column's type
1228      </para></entry>
1229     </row>
1230
1231     <row>
1232      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1233       <structfield>attstorage</structfield> <type>char</type>
1234      </para>
1235      <para>
1236       Normally a copy of <literal>pg_type.typstorage</literal> of this
1237       column's type.  For TOAST-able data types, this can be altered
1238       after column creation to control storage policy.
1239      </para></entry>
1240     </row>
1241
1242     <row>
1243      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1244       <structfield>attcompression</structfield> <type>char</type>
1245      </para>
1246      <para>
1247       The current compression method of the column.  Typically this is
1248       <literal>'\0'</literal> to specify use of the current default setting
1249       (see <xref linkend="guc-default-toast-compression"/>).  Otherwise,
1250       <literal>'p'</literal> selects pglz compression, while
1251       <literal>'l'</literal> selects <productname>LZ4</productname>
1252       compression.  However, this field is ignored
1253       whenever <structfield>attstorage</structfield> does not allow
1254       compression.
1255      </para></entry>
1256     </row>
1257
1258     <row>
1259      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1260       <structfield>attnotnull</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1261      </para>
1262      <para>
1263       This column has a (possibly invalid) not-null constraint.
1264      </para></entry>
1265     </row>
1266
1267     <row>
1268      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1269       <structfield>atthasdef</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1270      </para>
1271      <para>
1272       This column has a default expression or generation expression, in which
1273       case there will be a corresponding entry in the
1274       <link linkend="catalog-pg-attrdef"><structname>pg_attrdef</structname></link> catalog that actually defines the
1275       expression.  (Check <structfield>attgenerated</structfield> to
1276       determine whether this is a default or a generation expression.)
1277      </para></entry>
1278     </row>
1279
1280     <row>
1281      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1282       <structfield>atthasmissing</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1283      </para>
1284      <para>
1285       This column has a value which is used where the column is entirely
1286       missing from the row, as happens when a column is added with a
1287       non-volatile <literal>DEFAULT</literal> value after the row is created.
1288       The actual value used is stored in the
1289       <structfield>attmissingval</structfield> column.
1290      </para></entry>
1291     </row>
1292
1293     <row>
1294      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1295       <structfield>attidentity</structfield> <type>char</type>
1296      </para>
1297      <para>
1298       If a zero byte (<literal>''</literal>), then not an identity column.
1299       Otherwise, <literal>a</literal> = generated
1300       always, <literal>d</literal> = generated by default.
1301      </para></entry>
1302     </row>
1303
1304     <row>
1305      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1306       <structfield>attgenerated</structfield> <type>char</type>
1307      </para>
1308      <para>
1309       If a zero byte (<literal>''</literal>), then not a generated column.
1310       Otherwise, <literal>s</literal> = stored, <literal>v</literal> =
1311       virtual.  A stored generated column is physically stored like a normal
1312       column.  A virtual generated column is physically stored as a null
1313       value, with the actual value being computed at run time.
1314      </para></entry>
1315     </row>
1316
1317     <row>
1318      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1319       <structfield>attisdropped</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1320      </para>
1321      <para>
1322       This column has been dropped and is no longer valid.  A dropped
1323       column is still physically present in the table, but is
1324       ignored by the parser and so cannot be accessed via SQL.
1325      </para></entry>
1326     </row>
1327
1328     <row>
1329      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1330       <structfield>attislocal</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1331      </para>
1332      <para>
1333       This column is defined locally in the relation.  Note that a column can
1334       be locally defined and inherited simultaneously.
1335      </para></entry>
1336     </row>
1337
1338     <row>
1339      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1340       <structfield>attinhcount</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1341      </para>
1342      <para>
1343       The number of direct ancestors this column has.  A column with a
1344       nonzero number of ancestors cannot be dropped nor renamed.
1345      </para></entry>
1346     </row>
1347
1348     <row>
1349      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1350       <structfield>attcollation</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1351       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-collation"><structname>pg_collation</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1352      </para>
1353      <para>
1354       The defined collation of the column, or zero if the column is
1355       not of a collatable data type
1356      </para></entry>
1357     </row>
1358
1359     <row>
1360      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1361       <structfield>attstattarget</structfield> <type>int2</type>
1362      </para>
1363      <para>
1364       <structfield>attstattarget</structfield> controls the level of detail
1365       of statistics accumulated for this column by
1366       <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link>.
1367       A zero value indicates that no statistics should be collected.
1368       A null value says to use the system default statistics target.
1369       The exact meaning of positive values is data type-dependent.
1370       For scalar data types, <structfield>attstattarget</structfield>
1371       is both the target number of <quote>most common values</quote>
1372       to collect, and the target number of histogram bins to create.
1373      </para></entry>
1374     </row>
1375
1376     <row>
1377      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1378       <structfield>attacl</structfield> <type>aclitem[]</type>
1379      </para>
1380      <para>
1381       Column-level access privileges, if any have been granted specifically
1382       on this column
1383      </para></entry>
1384     </row>
1385
1386     <row>
1387      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1388       <structfield>attoptions</structfield> <type>text[]</type>
1389      </para>
1390      <para>
1391       Attribute-level options, as <quote>keyword=value</quote> strings
1392      </para></entry>
1393     </row>
1394
1395     <row>
1396      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1397       <structfield>attfdwoptions</structfield> <type>text[]</type>
1398      </para>
1399      <para>
1400       Attribute-level foreign data wrapper options, as <quote>keyword=value</quote> strings
1401      </para></entry>
1402     </row>
1403
1404     <row>
1405      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1406       <structfield>attmissingval</structfield> <type>anyarray</type>
1407      </para>
1408      <para>
1409       This column has a one element array containing the value used when the
1410       column is entirely missing from the row, as happens when the column is
1411       added with a non-volatile <literal>DEFAULT</literal> value after the
1412       row is created.  The value is only used when
1413       <structfield>atthasmissing</structfield> is true.  If there is no value
1414       the column is null.
1415      </para></entry>
1416     </row>
1417    </tbody>
1418   </tgroup>
1419  </table>
1420
1421  <para>
1422   In a dropped column's <structname>pg_attribute</structname> entry,
1423   <structfield>atttypid</structfield> is reset to zero, but
1424   <structfield>attlen</structfield> and the other fields copied from
1425   <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link> are still valid.  This arrangement is needed
1426   to cope with the situation where the dropped column's data type was
1427   later dropped, and so there is no <structname>pg_type</structname> row anymore.
1428   <structfield>attlen</structfield> and the other fields can be used
1429   to interpret the contents of a row of the table.
1430  </para>
1431 </sect1>
1432
1433
1434 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-authid">
1435  <title><structname>pg_authid</structname></title>
1436
1437  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-authid">
1438   <primary>pg_authid</primary>
1439  </indexterm>
1440
1441  <para>
1442   The catalog <structname>pg_authid</structname> contains information about
1443   database authorization identifiers (roles).  A role subsumes the concepts
1444   of <quote>users</quote> and <quote>groups</quote>.  A user is essentially just a
1445   role with the <structfield>rolcanlogin</structfield> flag set.  Any role (with or
1446   without <structfield>rolcanlogin</structfield>) can have other roles as members; see
1447   <link linkend="catalog-pg-auth-members"><structname>pg_auth_members</structname></link>.
1448  </para>
1449
1450  <para>
1451   Since this catalog contains passwords, it must not be publicly readable.
1452   <link linkend="view-pg-roles"><structname>pg_roles</structname></link>
1453   is a publicly readable view on
1454   <structname>pg_authid</structname> that blanks out the password field.
1455  </para>
1456
1457  <para>
1458   <xref linkend="user-manag"/> contains detailed information about user and
1459   privilege management.
1460  </para>
1461
1462  <para>
1463   Because user identities are cluster-wide,
1464   <structname>pg_authid</structname>
1465   is shared across all databases of a cluster: there is only one
1466   copy of <structname>pg_authid</structname> per cluster, not
1467   one per database.
1468  </para>
1469
1470  <table>
1471   <title><structname>pg_authid</structname> Columns</title>
1472   <tgroup cols="1">
1473    <thead>
1474     <row>
1475      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1476       Column Type
1477      </para>
1478      <para>
1479       Description
1480      </para></entry>
1481     </row>
1482    </thead>
1483
1484    <tbody>
1485     <row>
1486      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1487       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1488      </para>
1489      <para>
1490       Row identifier
1491      </para></entry>
1492     </row>
1493
1494     <row>
1495      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1496       <structfield>rolname</structfield> <type>name</type>
1497      </para>
1498      <para>
1499       Role name
1500      </para></entry>
1501     </row>
1502
1503     <row>
1504      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1505       <structfield>rolsuper</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1506      </para>
1507      <para>
1508       Role has superuser privileges
1509      </para></entry>
1510     </row>
1511
1512     <row>
1513      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1514       <structfield>rolinherit</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1515      </para>
1516      <para>
1517       Role automatically inherits privileges of roles it is a
1518       member of
1519      </para></entry>
1520     </row>
1521
1522     <row>
1523      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1524       <structfield>rolcreaterole</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1525      </para>
1526      <para>
1527       Role can create more roles
1528      </para></entry>
1529     </row>
1530
1531     <row>
1532      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1533       <structfield>rolcreatedb</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1534      </para>
1535      <para>
1536       Role can create databases
1537      </para></entry>
1538     </row>
1539
1540     <row>
1541      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1542       <structfield>rolcanlogin</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1543      </para>
1544      <para>
1545       Role can log in. That is, this role can be given as the initial
1546       session authorization identifier.
1547      </para></entry>
1548     </row>
1549
1550     <row>
1551      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1552       <structfield>rolreplication</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1553      </para>
1554      <para>
1555       Role is a replication role. A replication role can initiate replication
1556       connections and create and drop replication slots.
1557      </para></entry>
1558     </row>
1559
1560     <row>
1561      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1562       <structfield>rolbypassrls</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1563      </para>
1564      <para>
1565       Role bypasses every row-level security policy, see
1566       <xref linkend="ddl-rowsecurity"/> for more information.
1567      </para></entry>
1568     </row>
1569
1570     <row>
1571      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1572       <structfield>rolconnlimit</structfield> <type>int4</type>
1573      </para>
1574      <para>
1575       For roles that can log in, this sets maximum number of concurrent
1576       connections this role can make.  -1 means no limit.
1577      </para></entry>
1578     </row>
1579
1580     <row>
1581      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1582       <structfield>rolpassword</structfield> <type>text</type>
1583      </para>
1584      <para>
1585       Encrypted password; null if none. The format depends
1586       on the form of encryption used.
1587      </para></entry>
1588     </row>
1589
1590     <row>
1591      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1592       <structfield>rolvaliduntil</structfield> <type>timestamptz</type>
1593      </para>
1594      <para>
1595       Password expiry time (only used for password authentication);
1596       null if no expiration
1597      </para></entry>
1598     </row>
1599    </tbody>
1600   </tgroup>
1601  </table>
1602
1603  <para>
1604   For an MD5 encrypted password, <structfield>rolpassword</structfield>
1605   column will begin with the string <literal>md5</literal> followed by a
1606   32-character hexadecimal MD5 hash. The MD5 hash will be of the user's
1607   password concatenated to their user name. For example, if user
1608   <literal>joe</literal> has password <literal>xyzzy</literal>, <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>
1609   will store the md5 hash of <literal>xyzzyjoe</literal>.
1610  </para>
1611
1612  <warning>
1613   <para>
1614    Support for MD5-encrypted passwords is deprecated and will be removed in a
1615    future release of <productname>PostgreSQL</productname>.  Refer to
1616    <xref linkend="auth-password"/> for details about migrating to another
1617    password type.
1618   </para>
1619  </warning>
1620
1621  <para>
1622   If the password is encrypted with SCRAM-SHA-256, it has the format:
1623<synopsis>
1624SCRAM-SHA-256$<replaceable>&lt;iteration count&gt;</replaceable>:<replaceable>&lt;salt&gt;</replaceable>$<replaceable>&lt;StoredKey&gt;</replaceable>:<replaceable>&lt;ServerKey&gt;</replaceable>
1625</synopsis>
1626   where <replaceable>salt</replaceable>, <replaceable>StoredKey</replaceable> and
1627   <replaceable>ServerKey</replaceable> are in Base64 encoded format. This format is
1628   the same as that specified by <ulink url="https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5803">RFC 5803</ulink>.
1629  </para>
1630 </sect1>
1631
1632
1633 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-auth-members">
1634  <title><structname>pg_auth_members</structname></title>
1635
1636  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-auth-members">
1637   <primary>pg_auth_members</primary>
1638  </indexterm>
1639
1640  <para>
1641   The catalog <structname>pg_auth_members</structname> shows the membership
1642   relations between roles.  Any non-circular set of relationships is allowed.
1643  </para>
1644
1645  <para>
1646   Because user identities are cluster-wide,
1647   <structname>pg_auth_members</structname>
1648   is shared across all databases of a cluster: there is only one
1649   copy of <structname>pg_auth_members</structname> per cluster, not
1650   one per database.
1651  </para>
1652
1653  <table>
1654   <title><structname>pg_auth_members</structname> Columns</title>
1655   <tgroup cols="1">
1656    <thead>
1657     <row>
1658      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1659       Column Type
1660      </para>
1661      <para>
1662       Description
1663      </para></entry>
1664     </row>
1665    </thead>
1666
1667    <tbody>
1668     <row>
1669      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1670       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1671      </para>
1672      <para>
1673       Row identifier
1674      </para></entry>
1675     </row>
1676
1677     <row>
1678      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1679       <structfield>roleid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1680       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-authid"><structname>pg_authid</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1681      </para>
1682      <para>
1683       ID of a role that has a member
1684      </para></entry>
1685     </row>
1686
1687     <row>
1688      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1689       <structfield>member</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1690       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-authid"><structname>pg_authid</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1691      </para>
1692      <para>
1693       ID of a role that is a member of <structfield>roleid</structfield>
1694      </para></entry>
1695     </row>
1696
1697     <row>
1698      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1699       <structfield>grantor</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1700       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-authid"><structname>pg_authid</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1701      </para>
1702      <para>
1703       ID of the role that granted this membership
1704      </para></entry>
1705     </row>
1706
1707     <row>
1708      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1709       <structfield>admin_option</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1710      </para>
1711      <para>
1712       True if <structfield>member</structfield> can grant membership in
1713       <structfield>roleid</structfield> to others
1714      </para></entry>
1715     </row>
1716
1717     <row>
1718      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1719       <structfield>inherit_option</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1720      </para>
1721      <para>
1722       True if the member automatically inherits the privileges of the
1723       granted role
1724      </para></entry>
1725     </row>
1726
1727     <row>
1728      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1729       <structfield>set_option</structfield> <type>bool</type>
1730      </para>
1731      <para>
1732       True if the member can
1733       <link linkend="sql-set-role"><command>SET ROLE</command></link>
1734       to the granted role
1735      </para></entry>
1736     </row>
1737    </tbody>
1738   </tgroup>
1739  </table>
1740
1741 </sect1>
1742
1743
1744 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-cast">
1745  <title><structname>pg_cast</structname></title>
1746
1747  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-cast">
1748   <primary>pg_cast</primary>
1749  </indexterm>
1750
1751  <para>
1752   The catalog <structname>pg_cast</structname> stores data type conversion
1753   paths, both built-in and user-defined.
1754  </para>
1755
1756  <para>
1757   It should be noted that <structname>pg_cast</structname> does not represent
1758   every type conversion that the system knows how to perform; only those that
1759   cannot be deduced from some generic rule.  For example, casting between a
1760   domain and its base type is not explicitly represented in
1761   <structname>pg_cast</structname>.  Another important exception is that
1762   <quote>automatic I/O conversion casts</quote>, those performed using a data
1763   type's own I/O functions to convert to or from <type>text</type> or other
1764   string types, are not explicitly represented in
1765   <structname>pg_cast</structname>.
1766  </para>
1767
1768  <table>
1769   <title><structname>pg_cast</structname> Columns</title>
1770   <tgroup cols="1">
1771    <thead>
1772     <row>
1773      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1774       Column Type
1775      </para>
1776      <para>
1777       Description
1778      </para></entry>
1779     </row>
1780    </thead>
1781
1782    <tbody>
1783     <row>
1784      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1785       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1786      </para>
1787      <para>
1788       Row identifier
1789      </para></entry>
1790     </row>
1791
1792     <row>
1793      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1794       <structfield>castsource</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1795       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1796      </para>
1797      <para>
1798       OID of the source data type
1799      </para></entry>
1800     </row>
1801
1802     <row>
1803      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1804       <structfield>casttarget</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1805       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1806      </para>
1807      <para>
1808       OID of the target data type
1809      </para></entry>
1810     </row>
1811
1812     <row>
1813      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1814       <structfield>castfunc</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1815       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-proc"><structname>pg_proc</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1816      </para>
1817      <para>
1818       The OID of the function to use to perform this cast.  Zero is
1819       stored if the cast method doesn't require a function.
1820      </para></entry>
1821     </row>
1822
1823     <row>
1824      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1825       <structfield>castcontext</structfield> <type>char</type>
1826      </para>
1827      <para>
1828       Indicates what contexts the cast can be invoked in.
1829       <literal>e</literal> means only as an explicit cast (using
1830       <literal>CAST</literal> or <literal>::</literal> syntax).
1831       <literal>a</literal> means implicitly in assignment
1832       to a target column, as well as explicitly.
1833       <literal>i</literal> means implicitly in expressions, as well as the
1834       other cases.
1835      </para></entry>
1836     </row>
1837
1838     <row>
1839      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1840       <structfield>castmethod</structfield> <type>char</type>
1841      </para>
1842      <para>
1843       Indicates how the cast is performed.
1844       <literal>f</literal> means that the function specified in the <structfield>castfunc</structfield> field is used.
1845       <literal>i</literal> means that the input/output functions are used.
1846       <literal>b</literal> means that the types are binary-coercible, thus no conversion is required.
1847      </para></entry>
1848     </row>
1849    </tbody>
1850   </tgroup>
1851  </table>
1852
1853  <para>
1854   The cast functions listed in <structname>pg_cast</structname> must
1855   always take the cast source type as their first argument type, and
1856   return the cast destination type as their result type.  A cast
1857   function can have up to three arguments.  The second argument,
1858   if present, must be type <type>integer</type>; it receives the type
1859   modifier associated with the destination type, or -1
1860   if there is none.  The third argument,
1861   if present, must be type <type>boolean</type>; it receives <literal>true</literal>
1862   if the cast is an explicit cast, <literal>false</literal> otherwise.
1863  </para>
1864
1865  <para>
1866   It is legitimate to create a <structname>pg_cast</structname> entry
1867   in which the source and target types are the same, if the associated
1868   function takes more than one argument.  Such entries represent
1869   <quote>length coercion functions</quote> that coerce values of the type
1870   to be legal for a particular type modifier value.
1871  </para>
1872
1873  <para>
1874   When a <structname>pg_cast</structname> entry has different source and
1875   target types and a function that takes more than one argument, it
1876   represents converting from one type to another and applying a length
1877   coercion in a single step.  When no such entry is available, coercion
1878   to a type that uses a type modifier involves two steps, one to
1879   convert between data types and a second to apply the modifier.
1880  </para>
1881 </sect1>
1882
1883 <sect1 id="catalog-pg-class">
1884  <title><structname>pg_class</structname></title>
1885
1886  <indexterm zone="catalog-pg-class">
1887   <primary>pg_class</primary>
1888  </indexterm>
1889
1890  <para>
1891   The catalog <structname>pg_class</structname> describes tables and
1892   other objects that have columns or are otherwise similar to a
1893   table.  This includes indexes (but see also <link
1894   linkend="catalog-pg-index"><structname>pg_index</structname></link>),
1895   sequences (but see also <link
1896   linkend="catalog-pg-sequence"><structname>pg_sequence</structname></link>),
1897   views, materialized views, composite types, and TOAST tables;
1898   see <structfield>relkind</structfield>.
1899   Below, when we mean all of these kinds of objects we speak of
1900   <quote>relations</quote>.  Not all of <structname>pg_class</structname>'s
1901   columns are meaningful for all relation kinds.
1902  </para>
1903
1904  <table>
1905   <title><structname>pg_class</structname> Columns</title>
1906   <tgroup cols="1">
1907    <thead>
1908     <row>
1909      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1910       Column Type
1911      </para>
1912      <para>
1913       Description
1914      </para></entry>
1915     </row>
1916    </thead>
1917
1918    <tbody>
1919     <row>
1920      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1921       <structfield>oid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1922      </para>
1923      <para>
1924       Row identifier
1925      </para></entry>
1926     </row>
1927
1928     <row>
1929      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1930       <structfield>relname</structfield> <type>name</type>
1931      </para>
1932      <para>
1933       Name of the table, index, view, etc.
1934      </para></entry>
1935     </row>
1936
1937     <row>
1938      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1939       <structfield>relnamespace</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1940       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-namespace"><structname>pg_namespace</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1941      </para>
1942      <para>
1943       The OID of the namespace that contains this relation
1944      </para></entry>
1945     </row>
1946
1947     <row>
1948      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1949       <structfield>reltype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1950       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1951      </para>
1952      <para>
1953       The OID of the data type that corresponds to this table's row type,
1954       if any; zero for indexes, sequences, and toast tables, which have
1955       no <structname>pg_type</structname> entry
1956      </para></entry>
1957     </row>
1958
1959     <row>
1960      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1961       <structfield>reloftype</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1962       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-type"><structname>pg_type</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1963      </para>
1964      <para>
1965       For typed tables, the OID of the underlying composite type;
1966       zero for all other relations
1967      </para></entry>
1968     </row>
1969
1970     <row>
1971      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1972       <structfield>relowner</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1973       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-authid"><structname>pg_authid</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1974      </para>
1975      <para>
1976       Owner of the relation
1977      </para></entry>
1978     </row>
1979
1980     <row>
1981      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1982       <structfield>relam</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1983       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-am"><structname>pg_am</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
1984      </para>
1985      <para>
1986       The access method used to access this table or index.
1987       Not meaningful if the relation is a sequence or
1988       has no on-disk file,
1989       except for partitioned tables, where, if set, it takes
1990       precedence over <varname>default_table_access_method</varname>
1991       when determining the access method to use for partitions created
1992       when one is not specified in the creation command.
1993      </para></entry>
1994     </row>
1995
1996     <row>
1997      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
1998       <structfield>relfilenode</structfield> <type>oid</type>
1999      </para>
2000      <para>
2001       Name of the on-disk file of this relation; zero means this
2002       is a <quote>mapped</quote> relation whose disk file name is determined
2003       by low-level state
2004      </para></entry>
2005     </row>
2006
2007     <row>
2008      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2009       <structfield>reltablespace</structfield> <type>oid</type>
2010       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-tablespace"><structname>pg_tablespace</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
2011      </para>
2012      <para>
2013       The tablespace in which this relation is stored.
2014       If zero, the database's default tablespace is implied.
2015       Not meaningful if the relation has no on-disk file,
2016       except for partitioned tables, where this is the tablespace
2017       in which partitions will be created when one is not
2018       specified in the creation command.
2019      </para></entry>
2020     </row>
2021
2022     <row>
2023      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2024       <structfield>relpages</structfield> <type>int4</type>
2025      </para>
2026      <para>
2027       Size of the on-disk representation of this table in pages (of size
2028       <symbol>BLCKSZ</symbol>).  This is only an estimate used by the
2029       planner.  It is updated by <link linkend="sql-vacuum"><command>VACUUM</command></link>,
2030       <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link>, and a few DDL commands such as
2031       <link linkend="sql-createindex"><command>CREATE INDEX</command></link>.
2032      </para></entry>
2033     </row>
2034
2035     <row>
2036      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2037       <structfield>reltuples</structfield> <type>float4</type>
2038      </para>
2039      <para>
2040       Number of live rows in the table.  This is only an estimate used by
2041       the planner.  It is updated by <link linkend="sql-vacuum"><command>VACUUM</command></link>,
2042       <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link>, and a few DDL commands such as
2043       <link linkend="sql-createindex"><command>CREATE INDEX</command></link>.
2044       If the table has never yet been vacuumed or
2045       analyzed, <structfield>reltuples</structfield>
2046       contains <literal>-1</literal> indicating that the row count is
2047       unknown.
2048      </para></entry>
2049     </row>
2050
2051     <row>
2052      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2053       <structfield>relallvisible</structfield> <type>int4</type>
2054      </para>
2055      <para>
2056       Number of pages that are marked all-visible in the table's
2057       visibility map.  This is only an estimate used by the
2058       planner.  It is updated by <link linkend="sql-vacuum"><command>VACUUM</command></link>,
2059       <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link>, and a few DDL commands such as
2060       <link linkend="sql-createindex"><command>CREATE INDEX</command></link>.
2061      </para></entry>
2062     </row>
2063
2064     <row>
2065      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2066       <structfield>relallfrozen</structfield> <type>int4</type>
2067      </para>
2068      <para>
2069       Number of pages that are marked all-frozen in the table's visibility
2070       map.  This is only an estimate used for triggering autovacuums. It can
2071       also be used along with <structfield>relallvisible</structfield> for
2072       scheduling manual vacuums and tuning <link
2073       linkend="runtime-config-vacuum-freezing">vacuum's freezing
2074       behavior</link>.
2075
2076       It is updated by
2077       <link linkend="sql-vacuum"><command>VACUUM</command></link>,
2078       <link linkend="sql-analyze"><command>ANALYZE</command></link>,
2079       and a few DDL commands such as
2080       <link linkend="sql-createindex"><command>CREATE INDEX</command></link>.
2081      </para></entry>
2082     </row>
2083
2084
2085     <row>
2086      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2087       <structfield>reltoastrelid</structfield> <type>oid</type>
2088       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
2089      </para>
2090      <para>
2091       OID of the TOAST table associated with this table, zero if none.  The
2092       TOAST table stores large attributes <quote>out of line</quote> in a
2093       secondary table.
2094      </para></entry>
2095     </row>
2096
2097     <row>
2098      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2099       <structfield>relhasindex</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2100      </para>
2101      <para>
2102       True if this is a table and it has (or recently had) any indexes
2103      </para></entry>
2104     </row>
2105
2106     <row>
2107      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2108       <structfield>relisshared</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2109      </para>
2110      <para>
2111       True if this table is shared across all databases in the cluster.  Only
2112       certain system catalogs (such as <link linkend="catalog-pg-database"><structname>pg_database</structname></link>)
2113       are shared.
2114      </para></entry>
2115     </row>
2116
2117     <row>
2118      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2119       <structfield>relpersistence</structfield> <type>char</type>
2120      </para>
2121      <para>
2122       <literal>p</literal> = permanent table/sequence, <literal>u</literal> = unlogged table/sequence,
2123       <literal>t</literal> = temporary table/sequence
2124      </para></entry>
2125     </row>
2126
2127     <row>
2128      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2129       <structfield>relkind</structfield> <type>char</type>
2130      </para>
2131      <para>
2132       <literal>r</literal> = ordinary table,
2133       <literal>i</literal> = index,
2134       <literal>S</literal> = sequence,
2135       <literal>t</literal> = TOAST table,
2136       <literal>v</literal> = view,
2137       <literal>m</literal> = materialized view,
2138       <literal>c</literal> = composite type,
2139       <literal>f</literal> = foreign table,
2140       <literal>p</literal> = partitioned table,
2141       <literal>I</literal> = partitioned index
2142      </para></entry>
2143     </row>
2144
2145     <row>
2146      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2147       <structfield>relnatts</structfield> <type>int2</type>
2148      </para>
2149      <para>
2150       Number of user columns in the relation (system columns not
2151       counted).  There must be this many corresponding entries in
2152       <link linkend="catalog-pg-attribute"><structname>pg_attribute</structname></link>.  See also
2153       <structname>pg_attribute</structname>.<structfield>attnum</structfield>.
2154      </para></entry>
2155     </row>
2156
2157     <row>
2158      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2159       <structfield>relchecks</structfield> <type>int2</type>
2160      </para>
2161      <para>
2162       Number of <literal>CHECK</literal> constraints on the table; see
2163       <link linkend="catalog-pg-constraint"><structname>pg_constraint</structname></link> catalog
2164      </para></entry>
2165     </row>
2166
2167     <row>
2168      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2169       <structfield>relhasrules</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2170      </para>
2171      <para>
2172       True if table has (or once had) rules; see
2173       <link linkend="catalog-pg-rewrite"><structname>pg_rewrite</structname></link> catalog
2174      </para></entry>
2175     </row>
2176
2177     <row>
2178      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2179       <structfield>relhastriggers</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2180      </para>
2181      <para>
2182       True if table has (or once had) triggers; see
2183       <link linkend="catalog-pg-trigger"><structname>pg_trigger</structname></link> catalog
2184      </para></entry>
2185     </row>
2186
2187     <row>
2188      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2189       <structfield>relhassubclass</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2190      </para>
2191      <para>
2192       True if table or index has (or once had) any inheritance children or partitions
2193      </para></entry>
2194     </row>
2195
2196     <row>
2197      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2198       <structfield>relrowsecurity</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2199      </para>
2200      <para>
2201       True if table has row-level security enabled; see
2202       <link linkend="catalog-pg-policy"><structname>pg_policy</structname></link> catalog
2203      </para></entry>
2204     </row>
2205
2206     <row>
2207      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2208       <structfield>relforcerowsecurity</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2209      </para>
2210      <para>
2211       True if row-level security (when enabled) will also apply to table owner; see
2212       <link linkend="catalog-pg-policy"><structname>pg_policy</structname></link> catalog
2213      </para></entry>
2214     </row>
2215
2216     <row>
2217      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2218       <structfield>relispopulated</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2219      </para>
2220      <para>
2221       True if relation is populated (this is true for all
2222       relations other than some materialized views)
2223      </para></entry>
2224     </row>
2225
2226     <row>
2227      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2228       <structfield>relreplident</structfield> <type>char</type>
2229      </para>
2230      <para>
2231       Columns used to form <quote>replica identity</quote> for rows:
2232       <literal>d</literal> = default (primary key, if any),
2233       <literal>n</literal> = nothing,
2234       <literal>f</literal> = all columns,
2235       <literal>i</literal> = index with
2236       <structfield>indisreplident</structfield> set (same as nothing if the
2237       index used has been dropped)
2238      </para></entry>
2239     </row>
2240
2241     <row>
2242      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2243       <structfield>relispartition</structfield> <type>bool</type>
2244      </para>
2245      <para>
2246       True if table or index is a partition
2247      </para></entry>
2248     </row>
2249
2250     <row>
2251      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2252       <structfield>relrewrite</structfield> <type>oid</type>
2253       (references <link linkend="catalog-pg-class"><structname>pg_class</structname></link>.<structfield>oid</structfield>)
2254      </para>
2255      <para>
2256       For new relations being written during a DDL operation that requires a
2257       table rewrite, this contains the OID of the original relation;
2258       otherwise zero.  That state is only visible internally; this field should
2259       never contain anything other than zero for a user-visible relation.
2260      </para></entry>
2261     </row>
2262
2263     <row>
2264      <entry role="catalog_table_entry"><para role="column_definition">
2265       <structfield>relfrozenxid</structfield> <type>xid</type>
2266      </para>
2267      <para>
2268       All transaction IDs before this one have been replaced with a permanent
2269       (<quote>frozen</quote>) transaction ID in this table.  This is used to track
2270       whether the table needs to be vacuumed in order to prevent transaction
2271       ID wraparound or to allow <literal>pg_xact</literal> to be shrunk.  Zero
